summary: The post argues that “purity” in tech—especially Rust’s memory-safety absolutism—often shifts from technical rigor into ideology. Ronacher praises Rust’s innovations but criticizes how method purity can override practical outcomes, creating friction in ecosystems like the Linux kernel. He parallels this with the Python 2→3 migration, where purist stances dismissed pragmatic dual-support approaches and caused real community damage. Over time, Python 3 improved precisely because messy, incremental reality forced compromises. He concludes that a memory‑safe future is likely, but the path will be slow, impure, and shaped by lived constraints rather than doctrinal purity.
key_points: Rust’s memory-safety ethos can become an ideological purity test that values method over results, marginalizing practical exceptions like safe C or `unsafe`.
Ecosystems like the Linux kernel have different priorities, so Rust’s purity stance creates social and technical friction rather than effortless adoption.
The Python 2→3 migration shows how purity-driven shifts can harm communities and that pragmatic middle paths, though unpopular, often prove right.
Progress in language transitions is messy and iterative; real-world usage refines ideals into workable solutions.
Long-term change requires patience and acceptance that not everyone moves at the same pace.
money_quotes: What starts as a technical pursuit can evolve into something more ideological.
The fundamental question of “Is this code memory safe?” has shifted to “Was it made memory safe in the correct way?”.
This distinction matters because it introduces a purity test that values methodology over outcomes.
Not getting behind the Python 3 train was seen as sabotaging the entire project.
Python 3 is a much better language than it was when it first released.
The path to there however? That’s a different story altogether. It will be hard, it will be impure.
The painful and messy transition is here to stay, and that's exactly why, in the end, it works.
key_insight: Purity-driven ideals can catalyze innovation, but durable progress comes from messy, pragmatic transitions that respect real-world constraints.
